Novatian, De Trinitate 26–27: extended refutation of Patripassianism/Modalism; distinguishes Father and Son clearly
Mani's own teaching: two eternal, co-equal principles (Light and Darkness); attested in Kephalaia and Acta Archelai
Hippolytus, Refutatio 7.23: Theodotus taught that Jesus was a mere man adopted as Son of God at his baptism
Eusebius HE 5.28 (the Artemonite school continued adoptionist Monarchianism at Rome after Paul of Samosata)
Adversus Praxeam (Tertullian's magnum opus on the Trinity; entire work refutes Patripassianism)
Methodius, On Free Will; De Resurrectione: opposes Origen's universalism and apocatastasis explicitly
De Principiis 1.6.3; 3.6.6 (the eventual universal restoration of all rational beings to God)
Damasus's epigrams on Roman catacombs systematized the cult of martyrs; his Ferrua-collected inscriptions are primary evidence
Augustine's Contra Faustum (397), De Natura Boni (399), Contra Fortunatum (392): comprehensive refutation from a former Manichaean
De Lapsis (251) and the collected Epistles: the foundational African theology of post-baptismal penance and reconciliation of the lapsed
Hippolytus, Apostolic Tradition 34–38: the memento of martyrs at Eucharist; instructions for commemorating the dead
Hippolytus, Refutatio 7.23: Theodotus came to Rome from Byzantium and taught there; excommunicated by Victor I
Tertullian, Adversus Praxeam 1: Praxeas came to Rome from Asia Minor and persuaded the Bishop of Rome to withdraw recognition of Montanism
Tertullian, Adversus Praxeam 1: Praxeas later came to Carthage; Tertullian wrote Adversus Praxeam there
Mani spent his life in Mesopotamia; his headquarters were at Ctesiphon-Seleucia; he died in prison there in 274 CE
Augustine was bishop of Hippo Regius (modern Annaba, Algeria) from 391 until his death in 430
Augustine studied and taught rhetoric at Carthage (370–383); returned there before accepting the bishopric of Hippo
Ambrose was bishop of Milan (Mediolanum) from 374 until his death in 397; key see of the Western empire
Augustine was professor of rhetoric at Milan 384–387; baptized by Ambrose at Easter 387 (Confessions 5.13; 9.6)
Augustine, Confessions 5.13–14; 9.5: Ambrose's preaching resolved Augustine's biblical difficulties; Augustine received baptism from Ambrose
Methodius was bishop of Olympus in Lycia; later perhaps of Patara; martyred c.311 during the Great Persecution
Lactantius was imperial rhetoric teacher at Nicomedia c.298–305; witnessed the Great Persecution edicts there
Lactantius served as tutor to Constantine's son Crispus at Trier (Augusta Treverorum) from c.306
Eusebius was bishop of Caesarea Maritima from c.313 until his death c.340; the city was the seat of his historical and scholarly work
Lucian founded his famous scriptural school at Antioch; martyred 312 at Nicomedia; both Arius and Eusebius of Nicomedia were his students
Socrates HE 1.5; Sozomen HE 1.15: Arius was a student of Lucian of Antioch, who taught a subordinationist Logos theology that influenced Arius
Donatus Magnus became the Donatist bishop of Carthage c.315 after the schism; led the movement for over 40 years
